ground_truth.title: Top 30 Fine Art Photography Artists in the World
ground_truth.author: Sylvia Walker
ground_truth.main_content:
Today, photography is, without any doubt, the most accessible art form. Everyone owns a camera nowadays, as we all carry smartphones in our pockets or purses. We use them daily, capturing our surroundings in an endless stream of images. But what distinguishes an ordinary snapshot from fine art photography, and who are the artists defining this medium at its highest level, and in the past, and especially today? As a result, in this article, we will discuss what makes a photograph and artwork, recite eleven of the most important icons of the past, and present a reasoned anthology of the thirty most important fine art photographers today. 

Fine art photography is a form of contemporary art in which the photographer acts as an artist, using the camera not merely to record reality but to transform it. The intention is not to document but to create—to find meaning, emotion, or beauty beyond the obvious. The difference lies in intention: while a personal snapshot with our form preserves a memory or a moment in time, the artist photographer transforms or aims to capture the everyday into something more profound—from formal experiments up to more socially-engaged projects in a more documentary format. Furthermore, the editing—whether manual or digital—and the printing process are as significant as the moment the shutter is released. Through this, the photographer becomes not just a documentarian, but a contemporary artist and craftsman, shaping the image as deliberately as a painter or sculptor.

Before we turn to the living artists defining fine art photography today, it is essential to acknowledge the late icons whose pioneering visions shaped the medium as we know it. Starting with Alfred Stieglitz (1864–1946, American), who was a pivotal advocate for photography as an autonomous art form, elevating it from craft to modernist expression through his Photo-Secession movement and refined printing techniques. Man Ray (1890–1976, American-French) bridged Dada and Surrealism, transforming photography into a field of experiment and imagination through his cameraless “Rayographs” and avant-garde approach. László Moholy-Nagy (1895–1946, Hungarian), another radical innovator, integrated photography into the Bauhaus philosophy, using light, abstraction, and unconventional perspectives to redefine the visual possibilities of the medium. Ansel Adams (1902–1984, American) transformed landscape photography with his majestic depictions of the American wilderness, combining formal precision with environmental reverence. Walker Evans (1903–1975, American) brought clarity and quiet poetry to documentary photography, capturing the American vernacular with an honesty that would inspire generations to come. 

Henri Cartier-Bresson (1908–2004, French), the master of “the decisive moment,” distilled photography to its purest form—timing, geometry, and human intuition—laying the foundations of modern street and reportage photography. Diane Arbus (1923–1971, American) redefined portraiture with her empathetic and unsettling images of people on society’s margins, revealing the complexity of human identity and paving the way for more socially-engaged fine art photography. Robert Frank (1924–2019, Swiss-American) presented a raw, fragmented vision of postwar America, changing how photography could function as both critique and autobiography. Allan Sekula (1951–2013, American) merged image and theory, exploring labor, global trade, and capitalism’s unseen infrastructures with intellectual rigor and moral force. The duo Bernd and Hilla Becher (1931–2007; 1934–2015, German) catalogued industrial architecture with encyclopedic precision, laying the conceptual groundwork for an entire generation of contemporary photographers. Finally, Robert Mapplethorpe (1946–1989, American) elevated the photograph to an almost sculptural perfection, combining classical beauty with provocative content. 

Building on the legacy of these pioneers, we now turn to the leading photographers of today. The following selection presents the thirty most significant living fine art photographers, whose practices reflect the medium’s continuing evolution in the twenty-first century. While many contemporary artists integrate photography within broader multidisciplinary approaches, this overview focuses on those for whom it remains their solo, primary, or defining medium. As always, our top list has been created using the Artfacts algorithm, ranking artists based on objective data and career facts.

30. Dawoud Bey
Dawoud Bey (b. 1953, New York, the United States) is a leading American photographer and MacArthur Fellow whose work explores Black history, identity, and visibility in the United States. Since the 1970s, he has developed a practice that combines portraiture and historical reflection, creating deeply human images that recover and reimagine the Black experience in both contemporary and historical contexts. Bey began his career in 1975 with the series Harlem, U.S.A., which was first exhibited at the Studio Museum in Harlem in 1979. Over the following decades, his work has evolved to include large-scale color portraits, collaborative projects, and moving-image installations. 

Recent solo exhibitions include Street Portraits, Denver Art Museum (2024); Elegy, Virginia Museum of Fine Arts (2023–2024), traveling to the New Orleans Museum of Art; and Dawoud Bey: An American Project, organized by the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art and the Whitney Museum of American Art (2020–2022), which toured to the High Museum of Art and the Museum of Fine Arts, Houston. His work was also featured in Dawoud Bey and Carrie Mae Weems: In Dialogue, a traveling two-person exhibition organized by the Grand Rapids Art Museum. Bey’s work is represented in major public collections, including the Addison Gallery of American Art; the Art Institute of Chicago; the Brooklyn Museum; the Detroit Institute of Arts; the Museum of Modern Art, New York; the Solomon R. Guggenheim Museum, New York; the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art; the Studio Museum in Harlem; Tate Modern, London; and the Whitney Museum of American Art. Bey lives and works in Chicago.

29. James Welling
James Welling (born 1951, Hartford, Connecticut, the United States) is an American photographer whose work since the 1970s has continually expanded the conceptual and technical boundaries of the medium. Emerging from the experimental context of the California Institute of the Arts, Welling challenged photography’s traditional role as a documentary tool, focusing instead on the processes, materials, and perceptual effects that shape the photographic image. His practice spans abstraction, architecture, and landscape, unified by an investigation into how images construct meaning and perception. Welling studied at Carnegie Mellon University and the University of Pittsburgh before earning his BFA and MFA from the California Institute of the Arts, Valencia. Over the past five decades, he has developed a diverse and influential body of work that engages with both historical and contemporary approaches to photography.

Major solo exhibitions include Kunsthalle Bielefeld (2022); Musée des Arts Contemporains, Hornu (2021); George Eastman Museum, Rochester (2020); Stedelijk Museum voor Actuele Kunst (S.M.A.K.), Ghent, and Kunstforum Wien, Vienna (2017); Brandywine River Museum of Art, Chadds Ford (2015); and a traveling retrospective organized by the Cincinnati Art Museum and the Hammer Museum, Los Angeles (2013). Welling has received numerous distinctions, including the DG BANK-Förderpreis Fotografie (1999), the Infinity Award from the International Center of Photography, New York (2014), and the Julius Shulman Institute Excellence in Photography Award, California (2016). His work is held in major public collections, including the Centre Pompidou, Paris; Los Angeles County Museum of Art; The Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York; The Museum of Modern Art, New York; Solomon R. Guggenheim Museum, New York; and the Whitney Museum of American Art, among others. Welling lives and works in New York, the United States.

28. Graciela Iturbide
Graciela Iturbide (b. 1942, Mexico City, Mexico) is one of Mexico’s most celebrated photographers, known for her poetic black-and-white images that explore indigenous traditions, gender, ritual, and daily life across Latin America. Working primarily in documentary photography, her practice combines ethnographic observation with lyrical composition, offering profound reflections on identity and cultural continuity in Mexico and beyond. Initially studying film, Iturbide turned to photography under the mentorship of Manuel Álvarez Bravo, with whom she worked as an assistant in the early 1970s. Her distinctive voice emerged through projects documenting indigenous communities and rural traditions, such as her photographs of the Seri people in the Sonoran Desert and the Juchitán series depicting Zapotec women in Oaxaca—published in her influential book Juchitán de las Mujeres (1989).

Her photographs have been shown in solo exhibitions at institutions such as the Centre Pompidou, Paris (1982); San Francisco Museum of Modern Art (1990); Philadelphia Museum of Art (1997); J. Paul Getty Museum, Los Angeles (2007); Fundación MAPFRE, Madrid (2009); Fotomuseum Winterthur (2009); and Barbican Art Gallery, London (2012). Iturbide has received numerous honors, including the W. Eugene Smith Memorial Foundation Award (1987), the Grand Prize Mois de la Photo, Paris (1988), a Guggenheim Fellowship (1988), the Hugo Erfurth Award, Leverkusen (1989), the International Grand Prize, Hokkaido (1990), the Rencontres Internationales de la Photographie Award, Arles (1991), the Hasselblad Award (2008), and the National Prize of Sciences and Arts, Mexico City (2008). She also holds honorary degrees from Columbia College Chicago (2008) and the San Francisco Art Institute (2009). Iturbide continues to live and work in Mexico City.

27. Yasumasa Morimura
Yasumasa Morimura (b. 1951, Osaka, Japan) is a conceptual photographer, and also a filmmaker, whose work spans more than three decades. Using props, costumes, makeup, and digital manipulation, he transforms himself into well-known figures from art history, popular culture, and mass media. His reinterpretations of iconic works by artists such as Frida Kahlo, Vincent van Gogh, and Diego Velázquez examine the construction of cultural identity and Japan’s relationship with Western visual traditions. By assuming the roles of female characters and historical subjects, Morimura reconsiders gender and representation, questioning the conventions of portraiture and the dynamics of the gaze. His self-portraits merge performance and photography, creating images that blur distinctions between artist and subject, original and reproduction.

Morimura has held solo exhibitions at the Kyoto City Kyocera Museum of Art, Kyoto; Artizon Museum, Tokyo; Hara Museum of Contemporary Art, Tokyo; Andy Warhol Museum, Pittsburgh; Japan Society, New York; National Museum of Modern Art, Osaka; Pushkin State Museum of Fine Arts, Moscow; and Sammlung Friedrichshof, Zurndorf. His work is included in the collections of the Whitney Museum of American Art, New York; San Francisco Museum of Modern Art; J. Paul Getty Museum, Los Angeles; Museum of Fine Arts, Boston; Museum of Contemporary Art, Chicago; Museum of Contemporary Art, Los Angeles; and Carnegie Museum of Art, Pittsburgh. Morimura continues to live and work in Osaka, Japan.

26. Richard Mosse
Richard Mosse (b. 1980, Ireland) is an artist whose work expands the language of documentary photography to draw attention to overlooked but urgent global issues. Combining aesthetic and technical innovation, he creates immersive and collaborative projects that often employ specialized photographic technologies to make visible what usually remains unseen. Mosse has documented a wide range of subjects, including environmental devastation in the Amazon, the mass migration of refugees across Europe, the Middle East, and North Africa, conflict over rare earth minerals in eastern Democratic Republic of Congo, the US military’s occupation of Saddam Hussein’s palace complexes in Iraq, illegal immigration along the US–Mexico border, and the missing persons crisis in post-war Balkan nations.

Mosse has also worked in video, beyond his primary and photographic practice, which resulted in winning the Prix Pictet in 2018. His earlier project Infra (2012) focused on armed conflict in the Democratic Republic of Congo and was awarded a Guggenheim Fellowship. Mosse received the Deutsche Börse Photography Foundation Prize for The Enclave (2013), a 16mm film installation exhibited in the National Pavilion of Ireland at the 55th Venice Biennale. Mosse lives and works in New York, the United States.

25. Martin Parr
Martin Parr (b. 1952, Epsom, Surrey, the United Kingdom) is one of Britain’s most influential contemporary photographers, internationally recognized for his vivid, satirical depictions of modern life. “With photography, I like to create fiction out of reality,” Parr has said. “I try and do this by taking society’s natural prejudice and giving this a twist.” His distinctive style—marked by bright colors, sharp irony, and an eye for social nuance—offers a humorous yet incisive reflection on leisure, consumption, and communication in the contemporary world. Through his lens, the everyday becomes extraordinary, and the familiar takes on new meaning.

Over the past five decades, Parr has published more than 100 photobooks and edited another 30, cementing his place as one of the leading documentarians of his generation. His work is represented in major international collections, including Tate, London; Centre Pompidou, Paris; and the Museum of Modern Art, New York. A member of Magnum Photos since 1994, he served as its president from 2013 to 2017. Beyond his photographic practice, Parr has been an influential curator, directing major photography festivals in Arles (2004) and Brighton (2010). In 2017, he founded the Martin Parr Foundation in Bristol to support and preserve British documentary photography. Parr lives and works in Bristol, England.

24. Elina Brotherus
Elina Brotherus (b. 1972, Helsinki, Finland) is one of Scandinavia’s most recognized contemporary artists, working in photography, but also in video. Her practice moves fluidly between autobiographical reflection and art historical dialogue. Early works explored subjective experience and the relationship between artist and model, while later series shifted toward interpreting written instructions and performance scores from the Fluxus movement and other conceptual art of the 1950s–70s. More recently, Brotherus has turned her lens toward architecture, photographing herself within modernist homes designed by renowned architects. In these works, she inhabits imagined characters, her calm presence animating spaces that might otherwise seem static, creating a subtle tension between person and place.

Brotherus holds an MA in Photography from the University of Art and Design Helsinki (now Aalto University) and an MSc in Chemistry from the University of Helsinki. She lives and works between Helsinki, Finland, and Avallon, France. Among her many distinctions are the Artist Professorship Grant from the Arts Promotion Centre Finland (2022–2026), the Carte blanche PMU Prize, France (2017), the Finnish State Prize for Photography (2008), and the Prix Niépce (2005). Her work is represented in over 80 public collections, including the Centre Pompidou, Paris; Louisiana Museum of Modern Art, Denmark; LACMA, Los Angeles; and Moderna Museet, Stockholm. Brotherus has held numerous solo exhibitions at institutions such as MAPS Museum of Art in Public Spaces, Denmark; Museum Schloss Moyland, Germany; Didrichsen Art Museum, Helsinki; ARTER, Istanbul; Ateneum, Finnish National Gallery, Helsinki; and the Centre Pompidou, Paris. Her work has also appeared in major group exhibitions at the Bibliothèque nationale de France, the Biennale of Sydney, Brooklyn Museum, Istanbul Biennial, LACMA, Louisiana Museum of Modern Art, MAXXI, and The Photographers’ Gallery, London. Brotherus lives and works in Helsinki, Finland, and Avallon, France.

23. LaToya Ruby Frazier
LaToya Ruby Frazier (b. 1982, Braddock, Pennsylvania) is an American artist who also works in video, performance, installation, and publishing, with photography as her primary medium. Her work explores social justice, cultural transformation, and the lived realities of the American experience. Through collaborative storytelling with her subjects, Frazier addresses issues such as industrial decline, environmental justice, access to healthcare and clean water, workers’ rights, family, and collective memory. Rooted in the traditions of 1930s social documentary photography and the conceptual practices of the 1960s and ’70s, her work continues and expands upon these legacies to confront contemporary political and social inequities.

Frazier has been the subject of major solo exhibitions at leading institutions including The Museum of Modern Art, New York; the Baltimore Museum of Art; Brooklyn Museum of Art; Seattle Art Museum; Institute of Contemporary Art, Boston; Contemporary Arts Museum, Houston; Musée des Arts Contemporains, Grand-Hornu, Belgium; CAPC Musée d’Art Contemporain de Bordeaux; Carré d’Art – Musée d’Art Contemporain de Nîmes; The Silver Eye Center for Photography and The August Wilson Center, Pittsburgh; The Frost Art Museum, Miami; and the Musée d’Art Moderne, Luxembourg. Her numerous honors include a MacArthur Fellowship (2015), a Guggenheim Fellowship (2014), and a TED Fellowship (2015), as well as the Gwendolyn Knight & Jacob Lawrence Prize from the Seattle Art Museum (2013). She has also received honorary doctorates from Edinboro University (2019) and Pratt Institute (2017). Frazier lives and works in New Brunswick, New Jersey.

22. Lee Friedlander
Lee Friedlander (b. 1934, Aberdeen, Washington) began photographing the American social landscape in 1948. Known for his ability to organize complex visual information into dynamic compositions, Friedlander has created images that capture the humor and poignancy of everyday life. His subjects range from urban streets and dense natural landscapes to cars, trees, monuments, and nudes. His early photographs focus on the street, where he documented the intricate layers of the American social landscape through reflections, signage, and architecture. During the 1960s, Friedlander produced a celebrated series of self-portraits and The Little Screens, which depicted glowing television sets in mid-century interiors. Between the 1950s and 1970s, he also photographed jazz, blues, and country musicians—images later compiled in American Musicians (1998)—and created The American Monument, a series examining public statues and their surroundings. 

Friedlander’s photographs were included in the landmark 1967 exhibition New Documents at the Museum of Modern Art, New York. He received the Hasselblad Award in 2005 and has since been the subject of major retrospectives organized by the Museum of Modern Art, the Whitney Museum of American Art, the Yale University Art Gallery, Fundación MAPFRE, and LUMA Arles. His work is held in the collections of leading institutions including the Museum of Modern Art, New York; the Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York; the Whitney Museum of American Art; the Art Institute of Chicago; the National Gallery of Art, Washington, D.C.; and the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art. Friedlander lives and works in New York, the United States.

21. Boris Mikhailov
Boris Mikhailov (b. 1938, Kharkiv, Ukraine) is widely recognized for his groundbreaking photographic practice, which merges elements of cinema, documentary, performance, and writing. Over more than five decades, Mikhailov has served as a perceptive and uncompromising witness to the social and political transformations of his native Ukraine, documenting themes of war, displacement, and everyday life with both tenderness and critical insight. Originally trained as an engineer, Mikhailov discovered photography by chance and developed a distinctive visual language that combines social commentary with experimental technique. His work often embraces imperfection and chance, allowing the abject and the poetic to coexist within a single frame. Notable innovations in his practice include the use of hand-coloring and double exposure, as seen in his early series Yesterday’s Sandwich (1960s–70s), which reflects the contradictions of Soviet life.

In 1971, he co-founded the Vremya group, an underground collective whose experimental approach to photography laid the foundations for the Kharkiv School of Photography. He later served as head of the photography department at Panorama, the Ukrainian Union of Experimental Photography, from 1987 to 1991. Mikhailov’s work has been exhibited internationally since the early 1990s, with notable inclusions in the Carnegie International (1991) and New Photography at the Museum of Modern Art, New York (1993). He has held teaching positions at Harvard University and the Leipzig Academy of Visual Arts. Mikhailov has received numerous honors, including the Hasselblad Foundation International Award in Photography (2000), the Deutsche Börse Photography Foundation Prize (2001), the Goslarer Kaiserring Award (2015), and the Shevchenko National Prize (2021). His book Case History received the Best Photography Book Award at the Rencontres d’Arles (2000) and the Kraszna-Krausz Book Award, London. Major solo exhibitions of his work have been presented by Tate Modern, London; the Museum of Modern Art, New York; Berlinische Galerie; Sprengel Museum, Hannover; PinchukArtCentre, Kyiv; Maison Européenne de la Photographie, Paris; and Palazzo delle Esposizioni, Rome, among others. Mikhailov lives and works between Kharkiv, Ukraine, and Berlin, Germany.

20. Jochen Lempert
Jochen Lempert (b. 1958, Moers, Germany) is known for black-and-white photographs informed by his training in biology and long-standing interest in natural systems, phenomena, and time. He began using a 35mm camera during academic fieldwork across Europe and Asia and, in the early 1990s, shifted fully to artistic photography. Since 1995, he has pursued The Skins of Alca impennis, an ongoing series documenting the 82 surviving taxidermied Great Auk specimens. Lempert works exclusively with analogue methods, typically using a 50mm lens, enlarging with simple homemade developers, and printing on loose-weave paper that gives a soft focus. He embraces dust, lint, and torn or blurred edges, and often installs prints unframed in measured groupings to draw out visual correspondences among plants, animals, objects, and humans. 

Selected awards include the Camera Austria Award for Contemporary Photography (2017), Mutina “This Is Not a Prize” (2017), a Villa Massimo residency (2009), the Edwin-Scharff Prize (2006), and the Alfried Krupp Foundation Fellowship for Contemporary Photography (1994). Major solo exhibitions have been presented by the Centre Pompidou, Paris (2022); Portikus, Frankfurt (2022); Huis Marseille, Amsterdam (2022); C/O Berlin (2023); Izu Photo Museum, Shizuoka (2016); Museum Ludwig, Cologne (2010); Hamburger Kunsthalle (2013); Bildmuseet, Umeå (2018); CA2M, Madrid (2018); Contemporary Art Gallery, Vancouver (2016); and the Cincinnati Art Museum (2015). He was shortlisted for the Deutsche Börse Photography Foundation Prize in 2014. Lempert lives and works in Hamburg, Germany.

19. Andres Serrano
Andres Serrano (b. 1950, New York, the United States) is an American artist known for his large-scale photographs that intertwine themes of religion, corporeality, and mortality. Born to a Honduran and Afro-Cuban family and raised in a devout Roman Catholic household, Serrano’s work often explores the tension between spirituality and the physical body. He first studied at the Brooklyn Museum Art School in 1969 but considers himself “an artist with a camera,” stating: “I’ve never called myself a photographer. I studied painting and sculpture, and see myself as an artist with a camera. I learned everything I know about art from Marcel Duchamp, who taught me that anything, including a photograph, could be a work of art.” Serrano rose to prominence in the late 1980s with works such as Piss Christ (1987), a photograph of a crucifix submerged in the artist’s urine, which became emblematic of his approach—provocative yet rooted in reverence and conceptual inquiry. His images often incorporate bodily fluids such as blood, milk, or semen, confronting viewers with the visceral dimensions of faith, purity, and transgression.

Serrano’s work has been presented in major solo exhibitions including Retrospective, Moscow House of Photography (2005); Redemption, Fotografiska, Stockholm (2015); Torture and Ainsi soit-il, Collection Lambert, Avignon (2016); Maison Européenne de la Photographie, Paris (2016); Petit Palais, Paris (2017); Revealing Reality, Huis Marseille, Amsterdam (2017); The Game – All Things Trump, ArtX, New York (2019); and Infamous, Fotografiska Stockholm (2022). He has also participated in key group exhibitions such as Street & Studio: An Urban History of Photography, Tate Modern, London (2008); Traces du Sacré, Centre Pompidou, Paris (2008); An Incomplete History of Protest, Whitney Museum of American Art, New York (2018); and Incarnations – African Art as Philosophy, BOZAR, Brussels (2019). His works are included in major public and private collections such as the Museum of Modern Art, New York; Maison Européenne de la Photographie, Paris; Whitney Museum of American Art, New York; Brooklyn Museum; Israel Museum, Jerusalem; Vancouver Art Gallery; ARKEN Museum of Modern Art, Copenhagen; and the Smithsonian American Art Museum, Washington, D.C.. Serrano continues to live and work in New York, the United States.

18. Rineke Dijkstra
Rineke Dijkstra (b. 1959, Sittard, the Netherlands) is internationally recognized for her photographic, and often video-based, explorations of portraiture. Since the early 1990s, she has developed a distinctive body of work centered on young, often adolescent subjects, capturing them with clarity and empathy. Her large-scale color photographs and video installations employ minimal contextual detail, directing attention to the subtle dynamics between photographer and subject and the complex relationship between viewer and viewed. By doing so, Dijkstra’s portraits, marked by psychological depth and compositional precision, offer a contemporary reimagining of the classical portrait tradition.

Dijkstra studied at the Gerrit Rietveld Academy in Amsterdam (1981–1986) and has been honored with major awards including the Johannes Vermeer Prize (2020), the Hasselblad Foundation International Award in Photography (2017), the SPECTRUM International Prize for Photography (2017), and the Citibank Private Bank Photography Prize (1999). Dijkstra has been the subject of numerous international retrospectives and solo exhibitions, among them the Museum De Pont, Tilburg (2018); Louisiana Museum of Modern Art, Humlebaek (2017); San Francisco Museum of Modern Art (2012); Solomon R. Guggenheim Museum, New York (2012); Museum für Moderne Kunst (MMK), Frankfurt (2013); and Maison Européenne de la Photographie, Paris (2023). Her video installation Night Watching (2019) premiered in the Gallery of Honour of the Rijksmuseum, Amsterdam, underscoring her continued exploration of the portrait’s evolving place within contemporary visual culture. Dijkstra lives and works in Amsterdam, the Netherlands.

17. Nobuyoshi Araki
Nobuyoshi Araki (b. 1940, Tokyo, Japan) is one of the most renowned and controversial photographers of his generation. Araki has built a prolific and provocative body of work encompassing photography, film, and publishing, with over 500 photobooks to his name. His practice, which he calls “I-Photography” (shi-shashin), draws from the I-novel tradition of early 20th-century Japanese literature, embracing an autobiographical and confessional approach. Through this lens, Araki documents the intimate and everyday moments of his life—from his honeymoon and domestic rituals to loss, erotic encounters, and the cityscapes of Tokyo—blurring the line between personal narrative and social commentary. Over the decades, Araki has developed a distinct visual language that juxtaposes beauty and sensuality with decay and mortality, often combining motifs such as ikebana flower arrangements and kinbaku (Japanese bondage). His work challenges censorship and morality, engaging both Japan’s aesthetic traditions and its modern taboos. 

Trained in photography and film at Chiba University, Araki began his career as a commercial photographer for the advertising agency Dentsu before turning to more personal work in the 1960s. His series Sachin (1964), depicting children living near his home, earned him the Taiyo Prize and marked the beginning of his artistic career. Araki’s photographs have been exhibited internationally, including retrospectives at the Museum of Sex, New York, and the Musée Guimet, Paris. His work is represented in major museum collections such as the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art, the Stedelijk Museum, Amsterdam, the Museum of Contemporary Art, Chicago, and Tate Modern, London. Among his many accolades are the Photographic Society of Japan Award (1990), the Higashikawa International Photo Festival Award (1991), and the Mainichi Art Award (2012). Araki lives and works in Tokyo, Japan.

16. Edward Burtynsky
Edward Burtynsky (b. 1955, St. Catharines, Canada) is internationally recognized for his sustained investigation of the “indelible human signature” on the planet, caused by industrial incursions into the natural landscape. For over four decades, Burtynsky has created large-scale aerial photographs that reveal the sublime, abstract, and often unsettling beauty of human-altered environments. Referencing the visual language of painting, his images chronicle themes of terraforming, extraction, urbanization, and deforestation—documenting both the grandeur and the devastation of industrial progress. “Our dependence on nature to provide the materials for our consumption and our concern for the health of our planet sets us into an uneasy contradiction,” the artist notes. “For me, these images function as reflecting pools of our times.”

His works are held in over sixty major museum collections worldwide, including the Museum of Modern Art and the Guggenheim Museum, New York; Tate, London; Los Angeles County Museum of Art; Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofía, Madrid; and the National Gallery of Canada, Ottawa. Burtynsky has received numerous awards, including the TED Prize (2005), the Governor General’s Awards in Visual and Media Arts (2016), the Photo London Master of Photography Award (2018), and the Outstanding Contribution to Photography Award from the World Photography Organisation (2019). He holds eight honorary doctorate degrees and is a Fellow of the Royal Society of Canada and the Royal Photographic Society. In 2006, he was appointed Officer of the Order of Canada and in 2016 was named the recipient of the Governor General’s Award in Visual and Media Arts. In 2018, he was named Photo London’s Master of Photography and the Mosaic Institute’s Peace Patron. In 2019, he became a member of the Royal Academy of Arts. Burtynsky lives and works in Toronto, Canada.
with: Today, photography is, without any doubt, the most accessible art form.
Fine art photography is a form of contemporary art in which the photographer acts as an artist, using the camera not merely to record reality but to transform it.
Building on the legacy of these pioneers, we now turn to the leading photographers of today.
